region_of_origin: Pacific Ocean; widely consumed in Japan and internationally

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for raw yellowtail fish at this time.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw yellowtail serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one boneless fillet portion as served in sashimi or as a raw fish entrée.

nutrient_source.title: Yellowtail, raw — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy NDB 15236
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and secondary nutrients (sodium, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, dietary fibre, saturated fat, sugar) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Yellowtail, raw (NDB 15236). Core macro values from the input HPB Foodo record (cr_id F03010043, confidence 0.95) are consistent with USDA values and have been retained where they match. Fatty acid sub-fractions (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) sourced from the same USDA SR Legacy entry. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; GI not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Yellowtail, raw contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 and HPB Foodo (cr_id F03010043). Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. Per the Veritas GI rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index is set to null (not 0), gi_category is set to not_applicable, and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI value is assigned from the University of Sydney GI Database as the food category (raw fish, zero carbohydrate) is explicitly excluded from GI testing.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (39 mg/100 g) and high potassium (420 mg/100 g) with beneficial omega-3 fatty acids supporting endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Yellowtail, raw is an excellent choice for hypertension management: its sodium-to-potassium ratio strongly favours blood pressure reduction, and its EPA/DHA content supports endothelial function; patients should avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as regular soy sauce and ensure sashimi-grade quality for safe raw consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (23.14 g/100 g) with no glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yellowtail, raw has no carbohydrate or sugar content and therefore exerts no direct glycaemic effect; its high protein content slows gastric emptying and supports satiety, making it an ideal protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sweet marinades or glazes are avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA at approximately 1.56 g polyunsaturated fat/100 g); low saturated fat (1.27 g/100 g); cholesterol 55 mg/100 g within acceptable range
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellowtail, raw provides a favourable fatty acid profile with EPA and DHA that reduce triglycerides and platelet aggregation; its low saturated fat content and absence of trans fat make it a cardioprotective protein choice consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular disease prevention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with anti-inflammatory properties suppressing TNF-alpha and IL-6; low saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yellowtail, raw is rich in EPA and DHA which suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-6, directly benefiting joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; patients on immunosuppressants should ensure sashimi-grade quality and avoid high-sodium accompaniments that may worsen tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellowtail (Seriola quinqueradiata), also known as Japanese amberjack or hamachi (ハマチ) in Japanese, is a prized marine fish consumed raw as sashimi or sushi, or cooked by grilling, broiling, and teriyaki preparation. Raw yellowtail fillets have a firm yet buttery texture with pink-to-white flesh and a rich, mildly fatty flavour that is highly valued in Japanese cuisine. Per 100 g, raw yellowtail provides 146 kcal, 23.14 g protein, 5.24 g total fat (including approximately 1.56 g polyunsaturated fat with EPA and DHA, 1.64 g monounsaturated fat, and 1.27 g saturated fat), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a zero-carbohydrate, high-protein food. Its sodium content is very low at 39 mg/100 g while potassium is high at 420 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable electrolyte profile. Clinically, yellowtail is beneficial for hypertension (low sodium, high potassium, omega-3 endothelial support), Type 2 Diabetes (zero glycaemic impact, high satiety protein), and cardiovascular disease (EPA/DHA reduce triglycerides and platelet aggregation). It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Raw consumption requires sashimi-grade sourcing to ensure food safety, particularly for immunocompromised individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw yellowtail?
Is raw yellowtail low in sodium?
Can I eat yellowtail with high blood pressure?
What is the protein content of yellowtail?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 (Yellowtail, raw), an approved primary source. Values are consistent with HPB Foodo entry (cr_id F03010043, confidence 0.95). Fatty acid sub-fractions sourced from the same USDA entry. No estimation or analogy required for primary macros.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as yellowtail is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236 and HPB Foodo (cr_id F03010043). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant-derived fibre — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. Trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 based on USDA FoodData Central NDB 15236 which confirms negligible trans fat in raw yellowtail. Saturated_fat_g (1.27 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.64 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.56 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236; values are consistent with the total fat of 5.24 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_index set to null per Veritas GI rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 (data not available in consolidated form from the primary source for this iteration): vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These micronutrient and moisture values were not consolidated from USDA FoodData Central in this iteration to maintain source consistency; they are available in USDA SR Legacy NDB 15236 and may be added in a subsequent enrichment pass. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match assignments. SNOMED CT has no confirmed food concept for raw yellowtail at this time. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internally contradictory suitable=false flag despite describing strong anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefits; this has been corrected to concern_level=low in health_context additional_conditions.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Yellowtail, raw' is precise and unambiguous. Japanese local name present (ハマチ). Multilingual identity satisfied for the cuisine category (Global/Japanese requires Japanese script).

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 146.0, protein_g 23.14, fat_g 5.24, carbohydrate_g 0.0) are present and numeric. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236, an approved primary source. Zero carbohydrate is justified as naturally zero for a pure animal protein food, confirmed by USDA.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (39.0), saturated_fat_g (1.27), and sugar_g (0.0) are all present as numeric values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15236. All values are plausible and supported.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not 0) per Veritas rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category is 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source documents the rationale. This satisfies the q4 gate for zero-carbohydrate foods.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI (FOODON_03411433, yellowtail fish food product) and FoodEx2 code (A00LC, Amberjack and yellowtail Seriola spp., raw) are defensible exact matches. SNOMED CT gap is documented with an honest gap note.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation methods, taste and texture, cultural context (Japanese cuisine), clinical relevance (hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, CVD), and key nutrients including fatty acid profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (concern_level low, specific sodium/potassium/omega-3 guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (concern_level low, zero glycaemic impact guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (concern_level low, EPA/DHA and AHA guideline reference). Each has human_condition_name, concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 10 entries including English variants, Japanese script forms (ハマチ, ブリ), and romanized forms (Hamachi, raw; Buri, raw). aliases_by_language is populated for english, japanese, and romanized. All strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Self-check passed.
